title: APIs — How Programs Talk
subtitle: REST, JSON, endpoint design, and the rules of program-to-program communication

sections.title: What is an API?
sections.text:
An API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of rules that allows one program to talk to another. In web development, this usually means REST APIs — HTTP endpoints that accept requests and return structured data (usually JSON).

A well-designed API is intuitive, consistent, and predictable. If you know how one endpoint works, you should be able to guess how another works. This is called API ergonomics.

REST APIs use HTTP methods as verbs: GET /users lists users, POST /users creates one, GET /users/1 retrieves one, PUT /users/1 replaces one, PATCH /users/1 updates one, DELETE /users/1 removes one. Resources are nouns; methods are verbs.

config.caption: REST API endpoints: resources as nouns, HTTP methods as verbs

sections.caption: REST APIs use nouns for resources and HTTP methods for actions
sections.code:
# A RESTful API for users
GET    /users          # List all users
POST   /users          # Create a user
GET    /users/1        # Get user with id=1
PUT    /users/1        # Replace user with id=1
PATCH  /users/1        # Update user with id=1
DELETE /users/1        # Delete user with id=1

# Response format
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Alice",
  "email": "alice@example.com"
}

sections.title: REST vs GraphQL
sections.text:
REST — Resources are endpoints (multiple URLs). The server decides what data is returned. Over-fetching (getting too much data) and under-fetching (not enough, requiring multiple requests) are common problems.

GraphQL — Single endpoint. The client specifies exactly what data it needs in the query. No over-fetching, no under-fetching. But it requires a schema, resolver functions, and careful handling of N+1 queries.

REST is simpler and works well for most APIs. GraphQL shines when clients have diverse data needs (different mobile/desktop screens need different fields).

items: Client specifies exactly what it wants
One request can get nested data

sections.caption: REST vs GraphQL: different approaches to the same problem
sections.callout: Start with REST. It's simpler, well-understood, and works for 90% of applications. Move to GraphQL when you have multiple clients with divergent data needs.

sections.title: API Design Best Practices
sections.text:
Good API design makes the difference between a joy to use and a nightmare. Follow these principles:

  • Consistent naming — Use plural nouns: /users not /user or /getUsers
  • Proper status codes — 201 for creation, 204 for deletion, 400 for bad input, 404 for not found
  • Versioning — Prefix URLs: /v1/users. Never break existing clients.
  • Pagination — Always paginate list endpoints. Use cursor-based for large sets.
  • Error format — Return consistent error objects: { "error": "message", "code": "VALIDATION_ERROR" }

items.heading: Naming
items.body: Plural nouns. /users, /orders. No verbs in URLs.

items.heading: Status Codes
items.body: 201 created, 204 deleted, 400 bad request, 404 not found, 500 server error.

items.heading: Error Format
items.body: Consistent { error, code } objects, never raw strings.

items.heading: Security
items.body: HTTPS always. Rate limit. Validate + sanitize all input.

sections.caption: Six principles of good API design — consistency is king
